text: Hi, this is Dr. Mario Leba. I present to you a difficult case that I recently did. Patient had a prior history of tram flap and surprise mesh finding. I ended up doing an ETEP with unilateral tar with some tedious dissection along the way.

I tend to stay away from tram flap patients as the bulge is not going to get better. But in this case, There definitely was a defect and the patient was symptomatic. I looked at the CT scan and thought it potentially may be feasible. I did discuss with her all the potential complications and or outcomes at the end, which may or may not improve her symptoms. She elected to proceed.

As you can see here, I gained access to the retroactive space on the contralateral side, which would be the patient's right. My standard technique is blunt chokar on direct visualization. I make that space with a It works very well. Once I make the space, then I put my ports, dock the robot, and start the crossover. based on my initial quick peak port, identified that there was actually mesh in the abdomen, which I did not realize and the patient did not tell me. So I knew I was going to encounter some mesh during the crossover. In addition to that, I knew it was going to be somewhat difficult as the posterior sheath may be disrupted along with the tram flap subsequent changes and likely difficult dissection of that retroactive space. on the contralateral side.

The surgeon evaluates the crossover area, noting the abrupt end of the linea alba striations. The absence of the rectus muscle and anterior sheath reveals subcutaneous fat, which could be misleading. Muscle fibers are visible, possibly remnants from previous surgery, as the dissection continues near the defined medial aspect of the linea alba.

The surgeon identifies the mesh located medially and decides to approach the dissection above it to avoid the peritoneum. A vessel is identified and preserved, potentially feeding the tram flap. The surgeon finds a suitable dissection plane and maintains alignment along the linea alba.

The surgeon assesses the lateral extent of the dissection, carefully avoiding neurovascular bundles, which may have been compromised. The dissection is guided by the proximity to a previously identified vessel, ensuring the skin is not inadvertently punctured due to its close proximity.

Probably the most difficult part of the entire case, I thought, was the management of the very thin peritoneum. There's a lot of surgeons who want to do tap, I'm sorry, who want to do E-tept, but are not very familiar with the tap dissection and the concepts of peritoneal recruitment and gentle dissection, and this absolutely was present in this case. Majority of it was tap surgery and not necessarily E-tap surgery.

The surgeon uses a quick peak port to drain air and gain a visual window into the abdomen, revealing a hernia defect and a very thin peritoneum. Two linear strips of mesh are visible, possibly indicating previous surgical repairs. The surgeon assesses the trajectory of the dissection and plans to reduce the hernia sac before proceeding.

The surgeon identifies the edge of the mesh and begins the transversus abdominis release (TAR) plane using kinder dissectors. The mesh is elevated with the left hand, while the dissectors help maintain tension and soak up blood, allowing clear visualization of the dissection plane. The surgeon carefully adjusts tension to avoid tears, despite some occurring due to mesh adhesion.

The surgeon mobilizes the peritoneum, navigating past troublesome areas while the mesh is visible. There is disruption of the anterior tissue planes, requiring careful evaluation to ensure correct positioning. The surgeon uses intra-abdominal views through a rent and external patient assessment to maintain spatial awareness.

In the pelvic region, the surgeon attempts to locate the epigastric vessels to guide the trajectory towards the inguinal region. Several surgical clips are visible along the base, indicating previous vessel sacrifice. The round ligament is nearby, complicating the identification of the epigastric vessels.

After closing up all the holes and going as far laterally as I thought would be appropriate, then go in and measure for placement on my mesh. Now, the final part that was somewhat tricky and I'm not sure if I did the right thing but there's essentially nothing there's essentially a defect here and there's nothing but subcutaneous tissues above me and kind of thin skin generally you want to close all defects and even along the diastasis I thought about closing the diastasis but essentially no holes in it it's just going to add tension so then I was all well maybe I can try to close this mesh to whatever crappy tissue is present there and I gave it a shot but the mesh part was good and the ipsilateral side tissue was not very good and it just tore through so then I aborted that. probably could have forced the issue and gotten bigger bites and try to get that area closed. But I figured that tissue's already disrupted, that support is not gonna be there. And I just didn't think that I was gonna be able to add any benefit by doing that. I may be wrong here. However, at that time, that's the decision I made. And so far, the patient's been doing well.

Here I was just debating if I was gonna close the diastasis, and I just thought it'd add tension to it, so I decided not to do it. I've not done this in the past. This was a unique case. Others who've had ETEPs with maybe a tar on tram flap patients, I'd like to get your input on what you did. I really think there was some sort of injury at the initial surgery, and that's why they put those pieces of mesh in there, so the tissue planes were gonna be disrupted anyways.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for hematomas, with visible pooling of fluid in the area. The assistant PA is seen preparing the mesh while the surgeon uses suction to remove excess fluid.

The surgeon places the mesh, tailored to fit the defect, and applies VistaSeal for hemostasis and space inclusion. The abdomen is insufflated, and the surgeon confirms there are no breaks in the peritoneum or posterior sheath. A laparoscopic tap block is performed circumferentially on both sides to complete the procedure.
